lessons.title: std::jthread and Cooperative Cancellation

background.title: std::thread and the Forgotten-Join Problem (C++11)
background.content: `std::thread` from C++11 requires an explicit call to `join()` or `detach()` before the object is destroyed: if the destructor fires while the thread is still joinable (`joinable() == true`), the program immediately terminates via `std::terminate()`. This is a classic pitfall — an exception or early `return` can destroy the `std::thread` before `join()` is called, crashing the program. The RAII-wrapper pattern with `join()` in the destructor became so common a need that C++20 included it in the standard under the name `std::jthread`.

lessons.motivation: Multithreaded code written against C++11/14/17 suffered from two chronic problems. The first was the "forgotten join": if execution left a function via an exception or early `return`, the `std::thread` was destroyed without `join()`, triggering `std::terminate()`. The second was cancellation: there was no standard mechanism; developers invented flags, atomic booleans, and condition variables, reinventing the wheel every time. C++20 solves both: `std::jthread` calls `join()` automatically in its destructor, and `std::stop_token` / `std::stop_source` / `std::stop_callback` provide a standard cooperative-cancellation infrastructure that all standard library code understands — including `std::condition_variable_any::wait`.
lessons.theory:
`std::jthread` (C++20, header `<thread>`) is `std::thread` with two improvements. First, the destructor automatically calls `request_stop()` (if the thread has an internal `stop_source`) and then `join()`, eliminating the "forgotten join" class of bugs entirely. Second, if the thread function accepts `std::stop_token` as its first argument, `jthread` creates an internal `std::stop_source` and passes a token from it automatically — the caller does not need to do this manually.

**stop_source / stop_token / stop_callback** form the standard cooperative-cancellation mechanism.

`std::stop_source` is the owning end: calling `request_stop()` transitions the shared state to "stopped" and fires all registered `stop_callback` objects synchronously in the thread that called `request_stop()`. Calling `request_stop()` again is a safe no-op.

`std::stop_token` is the observing end: `stop_requested()` returns `true` if someone called `request_stop()` on the associated `stop_source`. Tokens are lightweight and copyable; they can be shared across threads without additional synchronization.

`std::stop_callback<F>` is an RAII callback registration: the constructor takes a `stop_token` and a callable `F`. If stop has already been requested when the callback is registered, it fires immediately in the constructor. The destructor deregisters atomically, guaranteeing that after the destructor returns, the callback has either already run or will never run.

**Usage pattern**: the thread function takes `std::stop_token st` and periodically checks `st.stop_requested()` in a loop. For waiting on `std::condition_variable_any`, C++20 adds an overload `wait(lock, stop_token, predicate)` that wakes up either when the predicate becomes true or when a stop is requested — no extra `stop_callback` needed.

**The relationship between `jthread` and `stop_token`**: every `jthread` owns an internal `stop_source`, accessible via `t.get_stop_source()` and `t.get_stop_token()`. The method `t.request_stop()` is equivalent to `t.get_stop_source().request_stop()`. The `jthread` destructor calls `request_stop()` before `join()`, so the worker thread is guaranteed to observe the stop flag on its next check or when it wakes from `condition_variable_any::wait`.

**Memory ordering**: `stop_requested()` requires no mutex; the shared state is stored in an atomic variable with acquire/release semantics. `request_stop()` publishes the work done by the requesting thread; an observing `stop_requested()` acquires that publication.

examples.title: jthread: automatic join and the stop_source/stop_token API
examples.expectedOutput:
--- jthread auto-join ---
worker step 1
worker step 2
worker step 3
scope ended: jthread joined automatically

--- stop_source / stop_token API ---
stop_requested before: 0
stop_callback fired
stop_requested after:  1
callback_fired: 1

examples.title: jthread passes stop_token automatically; condition_variable_any with cancellation
examples.expectedOutput:
--- jthread passes stop_token automatically ---
worker: cancelled
jthread done

--- stop_source + stop_callback ---
requesting stop...
callback 2 fired
callback 1 fired
stop done, stop_requested=1
callback 3 (late) fired
end

lessons.title: std::latch, std::barrier, std::counting_semaphore

background.title: std::condition_variable (C++11)
background.content: Before C++20, all thread coordination was built manually on top of `std::mutex` and `std::condition_variable` (C++11). A `latch` was a one-shot countdown barrier implemented as an `atomic<int>` + `condition_variable` + `mutex` + a ready flag. A `barrier` was the reusable multi-phase variant requiring even more boilerplate. A `counting_semaphore` was a concurrency limiter implemented via a `condition_variable` and a counter. All three primitives are now in the standard and implemented as efficiently as the platform allows.

lessons.motivation: Thread coordination repeatedly follows a handful of patterns: "wait until N threads finish initialization", "synchronize iterations of a parallel algorithm across phases", "limit the number of concurrently active threads". Before C++20, each pattern was implemented by hand using `mutex` + `condition_variable` — verbose, error-prone, and non-portable in terms of efficiency. C++20 introduces three high-level primitives: `std::latch` — a one-shot countdown barrier; `std::barrier` — a reusable phase barrier with a completion callback; `std::counting_semaphore` — a concurrency limiter. Each expresses exactly one pattern, reads like an intent, and allows the implementation to use hardware instructions (`futex`, `WaitOnAddress`) without the overhead of `mutex`.
lessons.theory:
**std::latch** (`<latch>`) is a single-use primitive: the constructor sets an initial counter `n`; each `count_down(k = 1)` atomically decrements it by `k`; `wait()` blocks until the counter reaches zero. `arrive_and_wait()` combines `count_down()` and `wait()` atomically. Once the counter hits zero the latch is permanently open: all current and future `wait()` calls return immediately. It cannot be reset — hence "single-use". Typical use: N worker threads do initialization work and each calls `count_down()`; the main thread calls `wait()` and only then starts processing the results.

**std::barrier** (`<barrier>`) is a reusable barrier for N threads. The constructor takes the participant count and an optional phase-completion callback (must be `noexcept`). Each thread calls `arrive_and_wait()`, blocking until all N have arrived. Once all have arrived the barrier runs the callback in one of the threads (implementation-chosen), unblocks everyone, and resets the counter for the next phase. `arrive()` (without waiting) and `arrive_and_drop()` (decrement participant count then arrive) give additional flexibility. A barrier is ideal for iterative parallel algorithms of the form "all threads process phase k, then all move to phase k+1".

**std::counting_semaphore\<Max\>** (`<semaphore>`) controls access to a resource: the initial permit count is set in the constructor; `acquire()` decrements it (blocking at zero); `release(k = 1)` increments it by `k` and wakes waiters. `try_acquire()` returns `false` instead of blocking; `try_acquire_for` / `try_acquire_until` support timeouts. The template parameter `Max` is the upper bound on the value; `std::binary_semaphore` is an alias for `counting_semaphore<1>`, convenient for one-directional producer-to-consumer signalling.

**When to use which:**
- `latch` — "wait for N startup events": N workers initialize, N network connections established.
- `barrier` — "synchronize iterations of a parallel algorithm": parallel merge sort, numerical methods by time step.
- `counting_semaphore` — "limit concurrency": database connection pool, limit concurrent HTTP requests.
- `binary_semaphore` — "one-way signal": producer-consumer without a queue.

All three primitives use `futex` (Linux) / `WaitOnAddress` (Windows) internally, avoiding unnecessary system calls when the wait is zero — unlike `condition_variable`, which always acquires a `mutex`.

examples.title: std::latch: one-shot countdown barrier
examples.expectedOutput:
All workers done:
  result_0
  result_1
  result_2
wait() after zero: returns immediately

--- single-phase sync ---
passed gate

examples.title: std::barrier and std::counting_semaphore / binary_semaphore
examples.expectedOutput:
--- std::barrier API demo (N=1) ---
after 1st phase, phase=1
after 2nd phase, phase=2
after 3rd phase, phase=3

--- std::counting_semaphore ---
acquired #1
acquired #2
released #1
released #2

--- std::binary_semaphore ---
consumer got: hello from producer

lessons.title: std::atomic_ref, std::atomic<shared_ptr>, Atomic Wait

background.title: std::atomic<T> (C++11)
background.content: `std::atomic<T>` from C++11 provides atomic operations on a built-in type `T` (integers, pointers). A variable of type `std::atomic<int>` is atomic in itself — you cannot use it non-atomically. Before C++20, atomics had three notable limitations: (1) you could not add atomicity to an existing variable (e.g. an array element) without wrapping it in `atomic<T>`; (2) `std::shared_ptr` had no standard atomic variant — only the free functions `std::atomic_load` / `store` (deprecated in C++20); (3) waiting for an atomic to change required a spin loop or a workaround via `condition_variable`. All three limitations are lifted in C++20.

lessons.motivation: C++11 laid the foundation for atomic operations but left gaps. Sometimes you need atomic access to an **existing** variable — for example, an element of a plain array — without rewriting all surrounding code to use `std::atomic<T>`: that's where `std::atomic_ref` helps. Safe sharing of `shared_ptr` between threads without a `mutex` required non-standard tricks; now `std::atomic<std::shared_ptr<T>>` is part of the standard. Finally, waiting for an atomic to change was typically done with a spin loop (`while (!flag) {}`) — wasteful for the CPU; `atomic<T>::wait` / `notify_one` / `notify_all` add efficient blocking wait directly on an atomic, without a `condition_variable`.
lessons.theory:
**std::atomic_ref\<T\>** (`<atomic>`) allows atomic operations on a plain variable `T` without changing its declaration. `std::atomic_ref<T> ref(x)` creates an "atomic view" of `x`: all operations through `ref` (`.load()`, `.store()`, `.fetch_add()`, etc.) are atomic. Meanwhile `x` remains a plain variable and may be accessed non-atomically when there is no concurrent access. The classic case: an array `int counts[N]` — different threads work on different indices, so an `atomic_ref` is constructed only for the relevant element. Important: while an `atomic_ref` exists, any access to `x` through other paths must also be atomic — otherwise it's a data race. Type `T` must be *trivially copyable*.

**std::atomic\<std::shared_ptr\<T\>\>** (`<memory>`) is a specialization added in C++20. Before it, the free functions `std::atomic_load(&sp)` / `std::atomic_store(&sp, ...)` were the only way to work with a `shared_ptr` atomically, but they required the pointer itself to be at a fixed address and were deprecated. Now `std::atomic<std::shared_ptr<T>>` is a full atomic type: it supports `.load()`, `.store()`, `.exchange()`, `.compare_exchange_strong/weak()`. The implementation may use a built-in spinlock or, when the platform supports it, lock-free instructions. Use case: the "publish a new configuration" pattern — a single `atomic<shared_ptr<Config>>` is read by many threads without a `mutex`.

**Atomic wait / notify** — C++20 adds three methods to `std::atomic<T>`: `wait(old)` blocks the calling thread while the current value *equals* `old` (analogous to `condition_variable::wait`, but without a `mutex`); `notify_one()` wakes one waiting thread; `notify_all()` wakes all of them. Semantics of `wait`: the implementation may use `futex` / `WaitOnAddress`, avoiding busy-waiting. `wait` accepts an optional memory-order parameter (`std::memory_order_acquire` by default). After waking, the caller must re-check the condition (spurious wakeups are allowed). `atomic_flag` also received `wait` / `notify_one` / `notify_all` in C++20.

**std::atomic_flag::test()** — before C++20, `atomic_flag` had only `test_and_set()` and `clear()`: there was no way to simply read the current value without side effects. C++20 adds `test()` — read the current value without modification, with configurable memory ordering.

**Memory orderings in brief**: `memory_order_relaxed` — atomicity only, no synchronization; `memory_order_acquire` — all writes before a `release`-publish are visible after an `acquire`-load; `memory_order_release` — publishes current writes; `memory_order_seq_cst` — total order, the default for all atomic operations. For simple counters without data dependencies, `relaxed` is enough; for data-readiness flags, a `release`/`acquire` pair is needed.

examples.title: std::atomic_ref and std::atomic_flag::test()
examples.expectedOutput:
--- std::atomic_ref ---
counters[0] = 2000
counters[1] = 2000
counters[2] = 2000
counters[3] = 2000
total = 8000 (expected 8000)

--- std::atomic_flag::test() ---
initial test(): 0
after test_and_set(): 1
after clear(): 0

examples.title: std::atomic<shared_ptr> and atomic wait/notify
examples.expectedOutput:
--- std::atomic<shared_ptr<Config>> ---
loaded v1: initial
after store: v2: updated
compare_exchange_strong swapped=1
current: v3

--- atomic<int> wait / notify_one ---
consumer woke, flag = 1

--- atomic<int> wait / notify_all (API demo) ---
notify_all called, val = 42
wait(0) returned immediately (val != 0)
done
